article_items.title: Robotics will not have a clean Llama moment
article_items.description: The Llama moment in robotics will not be the day a policy becomes downloadable. It will be the day another team can take that policy, adapt it to its robot, release it into a customer process, and still know what failed weeks later when the line stops repeating.
article_items.excerpt: The Llama moment in robotics will not be the day a policy becomes downloadable. It will be the day another team can take that policy, adapt it to its robot, release it into a customer process, and still know what failed weeks later when the line stops repeating.
article_items.summary: Skild AI raised $1.4 billion for an omnibodied robotics model, while Physical Intelligence is reportedly in talks for another $1 billion at a valuation above $11 billion. Bessemer Venture Partners has estimated the total global robot manipulation data at roughly 300,000 hours, compared with roughly 1 billion hours of internet video and 300 trillion tokens of text. What cannot be downloaded A real robotics Llama moment would need a team to take a model, adapt it to its action representation, validate it on its robot, and deploy useful behavior without support from the original model team. Even then, the team keeps the simulator calibrated against the hardware and watches for the point at which the real system has drifted beyond the model. DreamZero defines the architecture as a model that predicts future world states and actions from video. The useful data comes after the miss Robot data carries a different burden from language data.

article_items.title: Landmark German ruling declares Google's AI Overviews are Google's own words and makes it liable for false answers
article_items.description: A German regional court has ruled that Google is directly liable for the content of its AI search overviews. According to the court, previous limited liability protections for search engine operators don't apply to AI overviews. In this case, Google's AI had falsely linked two publishers to fraud and made claims that didn't appear in any of the linked sources. The ruling could set a precedent for AI-generated content liability worldwide.
article_items.excerpt: A German regional court has ruled that Google is directly liable for the content of its AI search overviews. According to the court, previous limited liability protections for search engine operators don't apply to AI overviews. In this case, Google's AI had falsely linked two publishers to fraud and made claims that didn't appear in any of the linked sources. The ruling could set a precedent for AI-generated content liability worldwide.

Google picks up 80 percent of the legal tab The court ruled in favor of the plaintiffs on most counts.
An analysis by AI startup Oumi for the New York Times found that Google's AI Overviews with the current Gemini 3 model answered correctly 91 percent of the time.
The Oumi analysis also found that 56 percent of the correct Gemini 3 answers couldn't be backed up by the sources Google linked.
Google covers 80 percent of the legal costs; the plaintiffs pay 10 percent each.

The court also found that the AI overview made claims "that are not even made in the search results." None of the linked sources drew any connection between the plaintiffs and the shady companies the AI mentioned.
The ruling may also have international reach, according to the court.

Google's own argument would also "significantly diminish" the benefit of the feature, the court noted, if the overview were "generally recognized as unreliable." The court also pointed to a protection gap.
The court treated the AI overviews as Google's own content and rejected Google's argument that users were responsible for fact-checking the results themselves.
The Munich court found that this reasoning doesn't apply to AI overviews.
According to the court, the AI mixed up information about other, genuinely sketchy companies with the plaintiffs and drew connections that didn't appear in any of the linked sources.

It's integrated with apps like Calendar, Maps and Gmail, helping you to automate day-to-day tasks like creating email drafts or checking location details.

article_items.title: A classic brain test exposed AI's biggest weakness
article_items.description: Researchers gave top AI models a classic attention test used in psychology and found a major flaw. While the models could correctly name colors in short lists, their performance deteriorated sharply as the task became longer and more complex. Some leading systems fell from over 90% accuracy to nearly complete failure.
